utterances: And I think what the Republicans try to do and were successful in bringing to the forefront of the political attention span of the country is the fact that there are people who seem more interested in using theatrics and more interested in using the political process than being deliberative and being that lovely body where we saw Jimmy Stewart making a stand, one honest man, one candid individual coming to Washington.
So I think we've seen more theatrics than we've seen anything else for the last 24 hours here.
What's the Republican strategy though?
I mean, the Democrats are - it seems, at least, trying to show, look, we're doing what we can to stop this war - what is the Republican strategy?
Can you even talk about a Republican strategy when you have some members, kind of, going off in a separate direction and saying, well, we don't really want this to continue either?

I'm hesitant to say that there is an overarching Republican strategy when you have senators, like Senator George Voinovich from Ohio and a number of other individuals, who are against what is going on in Iraq.

But for the majority caucus, the majority of the Republicans, I would say, Farai, they want to find a path to bring political stability in Iraq so that our men and women in uniform can come home victorious.
Mary Frances Berry and Ron Christie, thank you so much.
Thank you.
Thanks, Farai.

And what many of these Republicans are trying to demonstrate is what is the Democrat strategy other than pull the troops out or bring the troops home without hearing what the Democrats define success for being in Iraq, and how Democrats would define leaving the subjective behind in a victorious manner.
And I think that's what a lot of the rancor is.
As the Republicans say, we don't know what you're for.
All we know is that you're against the troops in Iraq.
All right.
I want to switch to a related, but separate topic.
Hillary Clinton just announced that former Ambassador Joseph Wilson is backing her bid for the White House.
He is the husband of the outed CIA agent Valerie Plame.
He criticized the president's pre-war claim that Iraq possessed weapons of mass destruction.
Mary, what is the senator's strategy here?
Why is she seeking or pronouncing this endorsement?
And why is she accepting the endorsement and announcing it straight.
It's because it helps with the anti-war people in the party.
It helps during the primary season.
The anti-war group is very large during the primary season.
And it gives her some cover for the people who argued that she wasn't strongly against the war even though everybody else was not strongly against it, to begin with.
And so that this is great for her because it turns out, of course, that Wilson was right.
There weren't any weapons - and we all know there were no weapons.
I guess we almost all know that there were no weapons of mass destruction now.
So it helps her with that particular group.
And it helps us during the primary season.
And that's where we are right now.
Ron, is there any way that this could backfire on her?
Mary made the point that this is the primary season.
But in the general election, should Hillary Clinton - Senator Hillary Clinton go to the general election?
She has high negatives among a lot of Republican voters.
Could this backfire in any way?
I think it could.
I think the good professor's analysis was correct.
I think that Senator Clinton is trying to appeal to the far left element of the base that is tired of the war in Iraq.
And Ambassador Wilson is viewed as a hero in many of these circles.
And she's trying to fill up the nomination for the Democratic Party.

But if she's successful on this, I think, looking for the general elections against whomever the Republicans decide to ultimately nominate, having the endorsement of Joseph Wilson who has been a very polarizing figure to many Republicans and someone who, even the Senate intelligence committee said that his testimony was less than forthcoming and accurate.

I think that he might serve as a lightning rod for Republicans to fundraise and to use and say that the Democrats and Senator Clinton in particular are using Iraq for political gains rather than going over there to achieve the objective of securing the peace.
I think by the time, Farai, we get to the general election - if we ever get there, by (unintelligible)…
(Soundbite of laughter)
There'll be so many lightning rods out there that Joe Wilson will be, sort of, a stick, not even a rod in the whole context of what's going on.
And right now, the fight is over who's going to win in the primary, and that's where we are.
Well, Mary, on that note, we have the O and O show.
We're talking Oprah and Obama.
Winfrey announced that in September, she's going to host a bash for Obama at her enormous California mansion.
Attendees have to give the maximum amount that an individual can give in a primary - that's $2,300.
If you want to hang around for a VIP reception, you have to raise at least $25,000 from other friends.
Is this a huge boost for Obama, Mary, and then, Ron?
Here is what I think.
I think that Obama can raise piles of money.
Oprah is already supporting him - we all knew that, I think.
And this is what she should do since she's supporting him - raise some money.
The problem for Obama is not the money; the problem for him is the votes and the polls.
And right now, even the local polls, I don't just mean the national polls.
The Iowa poll, for example, shows that she is now ahead of Edwards and some of them, primarily because of the women who vote for her - want to vote for her.
And she's way ahead of Obama in the early states.
So South Carolina is the one where he is most competitive.
So the real big question and what the turning point is going to be for Obama is the day when the polls start to move in the direction of the money.
But this has, like anything that Oprah does, it has all the glitter and all the hype and everything else, but it's exactly what she should be doing for her candidate and what she can do best.
Ron, what do you think about that whole point that a vote is not equal a dollar?
Well, I think that's true, but what Mary has said is right.
This is a goldmine for Senator Obama.
Senator Obama will be able to generate the funds necessary to be competitive in this early primary and caucus states.
And I think Oprah Winfrey has just generated an enormous groundswell - white, black, rich, poor across this country and an endorsement from her will go a long ways.

And Senator Obama will then have the tools and the money necessary to employ tactic such as getting on the airwaves in radio and television, and printing up very fancy and very colorful brochures to leave behind.
This is a great boon, a great feather in his cap, and he will be competitive.
And if I were Senator Clinton, I'd be looking in that rearview mirror because he is coming.
Mary, very quickly, what do you think could help Barack Obama try to reach out some of the states where there is not many black voters and apparently not a lot of supporters?
Well, he has to talk more about the issues.
I was out in Iowa when he was out there speaking at the Health Sciences Department.
And he had a lot of people who came to hear him speak and he gave the general - themes that he always gives but he never talked about anything specific.
And Iowa voters are very cerebral folk.
They sit around, think about who could win and what are they saying.
And they had all — the chat-up afterwards was, well, he was nice and he is great but in his themes the it brings- he didn't - what did he say about…
Mary Frances Berry is a professor of history at the University of Pennsylvania.
Ron Christie, vice president of the lobbying firm DC Navigators, and former special assistant to President George W.
Bush.
Both were at NPR's Washington headquarters.
This is DAY TO DAY.
I'm Madeleine Brand.
I'm Alex Cohen.
In a few minutes, a teenage singer-songwriter with serious angst and serious talent.
First, some serious disparities in sentencing for drug possession.
If you're caught with five grams of crack cocaine you could get five years in prison.
But it takes 500 grams of powdered coke to draw the same sentence.
Today, the Supreme Court took up this issue of sentencing disparities, and our legal analyst Dahlia Lithwick of Slate.com was there and she's here now.
Dahlia, how did the big gap in this sentencing guidelines occur?

Madeleine, it largely was really attributable to a lot of misinformation in the 1980s when the crack phenomenon was sort of new and it was all bound up with the war on crime and to get tough on crime.

There was just a misimpressions about what crack was, what it did - in a sense that it was far more lethal, far more dangerous, had much more pernicious effects in utero and so at that time the Sentencing Commission and the Congress decided to really crack down, so to speak, and go after crack all out of proportion to cocaine.
Of course what happened was this enormous disparity emerged, where cocaine users who are primarily white got much lighter sentences than crack users who are primarily urban blacks.
And so what we saw was this huge divide that began to happen although now we see that crack and cocaine are, at least in terms of effect, fundamentally the same.
Mm-hmm.
And Congress has tried to fix this over the years, right?
Well, the Sentencing Commission who's in charge of promulgating these laws and overseeing them has tried four different times to sort of realign the hundred to one disparity.
They put forth all sorts of different proposals and Congress hasn't really budged to accommodate them.
So what you started to have was a lot of district judges - sentencing judges who said I'm essentially going to take this into my own hands.
I will say that the Sentencing Commission has made a tweak to the hundred to the hundred to one ratio that will go into effect in November unless Congress stops it.
So this is going to change a little bit but this was, in some sense, judges sort of engaging in a little bit of self-help.
Well, tell us today about the cases that the court heard.
It was a pair of cases - one of then was sort of touched on this crack-cocaine disparity.

Both of them - the thing they had in common was that they were about the court sentencing doctrine, which has kind of had a quite revolution that culminated in about 2005 in the case of the United States v.
Booker.
That was a case that said the federal sentencing guidelines that used to be mandatory guidelines had become, quote, ""advisory.
Really, the question has become now, how advisory is advisory?
And what the court has established, as recently as last year, is that if judges stay within the guidelines, there is this presumption that the sentence is probably reasonable.
What happens though, this is the corollary question when the judges deviate way out of the guidelines, as the two cases today.
So we solve one case that had a sort of Iowa college student selling a lot of ecstasy for a couple of months in 2000, makes a whole bunch of money, withdraws from the conspiracy.
The judge says, I'm not going to go with this pretty extreme 30 to 37 months of imprisonment under the guidelines; I'm going to go all the way down to probation.
Three years of probation, and he was overturned on the 8th Circuit.
The other case, as we've sort of hinted, had to do with the Virginia case.

Again, someone who got a sentence that the judge just determined was way too extreme because he didn't really have a policy sense that the crack cocaine disparity was right - and so both of these cases come up as sentencing cases asking again this very fundamental question.
Look, if the sentencing guidelines are in fact advisory - if judges are allowed to deviate out, how much is too much advisory?
How much is too much deviation and how do we measure when judges have gone too far?
And, Dahlia, this is the second day of the new term, anything new from what you've seen in court so far?
Well, you know, interesting cases certainly, Madeleine, but I think the big news is all happening in a sort of gossip world.
You know, we've seen a bunch of very, very explosive reports on what goes on behind the scenes at the courts.
We had Jeffrey Toobin's big book that's just come out to tell us all sorts of back-story and scuttlebutt about what's going on.
And then just yesterday, Justice Clarence Thomas' major book came out, his autobiography.

And so I think in some sense we now know an awful lot about the court that has nothing to do with footnotes and dictum and holdings, and lot to do with, you know, personal reputations and revelations and maybe even a little bit more than we really feel like need to know.
Well, TMI there at the court.
Dahlia Lithwick of Slate.com, thank you very much.
It's always a pleasure.
Lance Armstrong won the Tour de France for a record-setting seven consecutive years.
But how many people remember the name Marshall Major Taylor?
